questions.question: What is the key difference between first and second generation repeaters?

options.text: Second generation uses encoded qubits for deterministic operations
Second generation requires no quantum memories

questions.explanation: Second-generation repeaters encode logical qubits in quantum error-correcting codes, enabling deterministic Bell measurements and eliminating the probabilistic swapping penalty of first-generation systems.

questions.question: Why does encoding enable deterministic BSM?

options.text: Logical CNOT gates can distinguish all four Bell states
Encoding removes the need for measurement

questions.explanation: With encoded qubits, logical CNOT gates followed by logical measurements can distinguish all four Bell states. This circumvents the linear optics limitation that restricts physical photonic BSM to 50% success.

questions.question: What replaces purification in second-generation repeaters?
questions.explanation: Quantum error-correcting codes detect and correct errors during transmission and operation, replacing the probabilistic purification of first-generation repeaters with deterministic error correction.

questions.question: What is a disadvantage of second-generation compared to first-generation?

options.text: Requires many more physical qubits per node

questions.explanation: Second-generation repeaters require encoding each logical qubit in many physical qubits (7+ for Steane code, 25+ for surface codes). This increases complexity and resource requirements at each node.

questions.question: What is a one-way quantum repeater?

options.text: A repeater that only works in one direction
A repeater using measurement-based quantum computation
A repeater that sends qubits but never receives

questions.explanation: One-way repeaters use cluster states and adaptive measurements (measurement-based QC paradigm). The 'one-way' refers to the computation model, not the direction of communication.

questions.question: How does encoding help with photon loss?

options.text: Loss-tolerant codes can reconstruct state from partial arrivals
Encoding prevents photons from being lost
Encoding makes photons invisible to the fiber

questions.explanation: Loss-tolerant quantum codes (like quantum parity codes) can reconstruct the encoded state even when some physical qubits are lost. This converts deterministic loss into erasure errors that can be corrected.

questions.question: Second-generation repeaters completely eliminate the need for quantum memories.
questions.explanation: False. Second-generation repeaters still need quantum memories to store encoded qubits while awaiting swapping operations or synchronized with other segments. The memories must now store encoded logical qubits.
